title: Knowledge and experience sharing session (09/05/2026)
content:
Knowledge and experience sharing session Report — 09/05/2026 Event Purpose Share knowledge about AWS, AI, and soft skills within the First Cloud Journey / FCAJ community. Connect learners at the AWS Vietnam office. Event Information Event Name: Knowledge and experience sharing session — First Cloud Journey AI Date: 09/05/2026 Location: 26th Floor - AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Event Photos: LinkedIn Post Speakers / Sessions 1. Huynh Long - Learning Experience Huynh Long shared about personal learning methods, how he sets goals and determines the achievements to be reached after each study session. At the same time, he emphasized the importance of summarizing practical experiences and constantly striving to conquer the set goals. 2. Thinh Nguyen - Proptimizer Thinh Nguyen shared about Proptimizer — an AI prompt optimization tool that helps improve the efficiency and output quality of Large Language Models (LLMs). 3. Khang Nguyen - Growth Mindset Khang Nguyen shared about Growth Mindset, how to practice the habit of always asking “Why?” and viewing curiosity as one’s greatest asset. At the same time, he emphasized the importance of daring to accept mistakes as valuable lessons, and always maintaining a relentless desire to learn so as not to be left behind. 4. Nguyen Phuong Thao - Developer Agent Nguyen Phuong Thao shared about “Developer Agent” models and discipline when coding for each individual Story. She introduced a methodical workflow, from breaking down detailed tasks to accurate code execution, in order to optimize performance and ensure quality before submitting for review. Group Photo Takeaways & Application Growth Mindset & Self-study Discipline: Practice the habit of asking “Why?” and self-summarize achievements after each study session to continuously improve instead of fearing mistakes. AI Prompt Optimization: Apply the mindset from the Proptimizer tool to improve output quality when communicating with LLM models. Developer Agent & Code Discipline: Break down Stories into extremely detailed subtasks, code definitively, and avoid guessing (useful to avoid conflicts on GitHub/GitLab when developing features for projects like WebFood). Event Experience The event brought a variety of diverse presentations: from learning methods, personal growth mindset, AI tools, to the actual workflow of a developer. The atmosphere at the AWS Vietnam office (Bitexco Tower) was very professional, creating a clear sense of FCAJ community connection.
Overall, the event helped me broaden my multi-dimensional perspective — from sharpening self-study mindset and optimizing AI tools, to applying methodical programming discipline into practical projects.

title: Blog 1 — AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock
content:
AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock & AgentCore — AI that does work, not just answers Hi everyone,
While learning about AWS and Generative AI, I often read AWS Blog posts to understand how companies apply technology in practice. Recently I studied how AWS builds AI Agent systems with Amazon Bedrock and AgentCore, and found this approach especially interesting.
At first I expected these articles to focus on AI models or LLM capability. After digging deeper, the most valuable insight was not the model itself, but how AWS designs a system that lets AI actually perform work.
An AI Agent is not just a chatbot Previously, when I thought about AI, I usually imagined chatbots or Q&A systems.
With AI Agent architecture on AWS, AI goes beyond answering questions. It can:
Understand goals Plan Execute steps Observe results Adjust actions continuously This makes AI behave more like a system operator than a simple assistant tool.
System architecture: AI does not do everything alone What I found most interesting is how AWS designs the architecture.
AI does not handle the entire system directly. It sits inside a clear flow:
The user sends a request through CloudFront + API Gateway Lambda handles intermediate logic The request enters the AgentCore Runtime An Orchestrator Agent coordinates sub-agents Those agents use Tools (APIs / services) to take action The key idea: AI does not replace the system — it works with the system through APIs.
Multi-Agent: Split work like a real team Another strong point is that the system does not rely on a single AI. It uses multiple agents:
A Find agent for information gathering A Create agent for planning An Execute agent for taking action This mirrors a real team: analyst, designer, and implementer. As a result, the system is easier to scale, debug, and reason about.
Tool Use — the most important key My main takeaway: AI becomes truly useful only when it can use tools.
In this architecture:
AI does not operate the system directly Every action goes through APIs, databases, or external services This makes the system more stable, easier to swap AI models, and less dependent on one technology.
Applications go beyond AI or DevOps Once I understood the model, I realized it can apply to many use cases:
Automated website / app testing (like QA) Automated support ticket handling DevOps automation (deploy, monitor) Smart data pipelines Business AI assistants By changing only the tools an agent is allowed to use, the same system can be reused.
Personal perspective The most important lesson for me: the value of AI is not “smart answers,” but getting real work done.
In this system, Amazon Bedrock is not only a place to run models. It is a platform for building AI Agents, connecting them to systems, and automating workflows.
This completely changed how I see AI: not just a tool → but a component inside the system.
Conclusion For me, this is not merely an AI architecture. It is a new approach to building systems:
AI does not stand alone AI does not replace the system AI becomes a worker inside the system If you are learning about Amazon Bedrock, AI Agents, or Generative AI on AWS, this is a direction worth studying — because it shows how the technology is applied in practice, not only in demos.
Facebook post link:
https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/permalink/2210158863082407/

title: DynamoDB
content:
DynamoDB Console: DynamoDB → Create table webfood-connections-prod
Partition key: connectionId (String) GSI: byUserId with partition key userId Capacity: On-demand; enable TTL attribute ttl Next: 5.4.2 SQS.

title: EventBridge Rules
content:
EventBridge Rules Console: EventBridge → Rules → select bus webfood-events-prod → Create rule (twice).
Rule 1 — webfood-to-queue-prod → SQS Worker Field Value Name webfood-to-queue-prod Event bus webfood-events-prod Rule type Rule with an event pattern Event pattern (JSON) see below { "detail-type": ["OrderCreated", "OrderStatusChanged", "PaymentSucceeded"] } → Target: SQS queue webfood-order-events-prod → Create rule.
Rule 2 — webfood-to-ws-prod → Lambda WsNotify Field Value Name webfood-to-ws-prod Event bus webfood-events-prod Event pattern (JSON) see below { "detail-type": ["OrderCreated", "OrderStatusChanged", "PaymentSucceeded", "VoucherPublished"] } → Target: Lambda function webfood-ws-notify → Create rule.
Console adds EventBridge invoke permission automatically. If missing: Lambda webfood-ws-notify → Permissions → principal events.amazonaws.com.
Next: 5.6.2 REST API.

title: IAM Role
content:
IAM Role for Lambda Console: IAM → Roles → Create role webfood-lambda-role-prod
Attach AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole, AWSXRayDaemonWriteAccess and inline policy (Secrets Manager, S3, EventBridge, DynamoDB, SES, SQS, WebSocket).
Next: 5.5.2 Lambda Functions.

title: Overview
content:
Live deployed demo Reviewers can open the running WebFood system at:
https://dh9xhc3jrccu4.cloudfront.net/
This is the CloudFront distribution for the project (frontend + API) after completing the workshop steps.
Workshop Objectives After completing this workshop, you will:
Deploy the full WebFood platform on AWS Serverless. Understand the request flow from CloudFront → API Gateway → Lambda → MongoDB Atlas. Configure event-driven pipeline: EventBridge → SQS/Worker/SES and WebSocket notify. Operate React frontend on S3 + CloudFront with WAF protection. Architecture Overview WebFood uses a serverless model: no EC2, all compute runs on Lambda. Frontend is a React SPA built statically and hosted on S3, distributed via CloudFront.
Main Processing Flows Order (REST): Browser → CloudFront /api/* → API Gateway REST → Lambda webfood-api → MongoDB → publish EventBridge. Email (async): EventBridge rule → SQS → Lambda webfood-worker → SES sends confirmation email. Real-time (WebSocket): EventBridge rule → Lambda webfood-ws-notify → API Gateway WebSocket → client receives voucher/order toast. WS Connection: Client opens WebSocket → Lambda webfood-ws-connect → saves connectionId to DynamoDB. Lambda Functions Function Handler Role webfood-api src/lambda.handler REST API (Express app) webfood-worker src/lambda-worker.handler Process SQS, send SES email webfood-ws-connect src/lambda-ws-connect.handler Route $connect webfood-ws-disconnect src/lambda-ws-disconnect.handler Route $disconnect webfood-ws-notify src/lambda-ws-notify.handler Push message via WebSocket EventBridge Event Types OrderCreated — new order OrderStatusChanged — status update PaymentSucceeded — successful MoMo payment VoucherPublished — admin publishes voucher (WebSocket only) Workshop Roadmap Section Contents 5.2 Prerequisites Accounts, tools 5.3 Secrets & Storage Secrets Manager, S3 5.4 Data & Messaging DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS 5.5 Lambda IAM, functions, SQS trigger 5.6 API & Events Rules, REST, WebSocket 5.7 CDN & Operations WAF, CloudFront, SES, frontend 5.8 Smoke Test Smoke test 5.9 Teardown Delete resources

title: Secrets Manager
content:
Secrets Manager Console: search “Secrets Manager” → Store a new secret
Secret type: Other type of secret Plaintext tab, paste application JSON (MONGO_URI, JWT, MoMo, SES…) Secret name: webfood/prod/app-secrets → Store 📌 Note the Secret ARN Next: 5.3.2 S3 Buckets.

title: WAF
content:
WAF WebACL Console: WAF & Shield → Create web ACL webfood-waf-prod for CloudFront.
Add managed rules: AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet.
Next: 5.7.2 CloudFront.

title: Worklog
content:
On this page, you will need to introduce your worklog. How did you complete it? How many weeks did you take to complete the program? What did you do in those weeks?
Typically, and as a standard, a worklog is carried out over about 3 months (throughout the internship period) with weekly contents as follows:
Week 1: Introduction to AWS Cloud & EC2
Week 2: Amazon S3 and IAM
Week 3: Amazon VPC and Networking
Week 4: Amazon RDS and CloudWatch
Week 5: AWS Lambda and API Gateway
Week 6: Docker and Amazon ECS
Week 7: Infrastructure as Code and CI/CD
Week 8: Event-driven Architecture and Amazon EventBridge
Week 9: WebSocket, DynamoDB and Amazon SES
Week 10: CloudFront, AWS WAF and Monitoring
Week 11: Development and Integration of WebFood Project
Week 12: Testing, Optimization, and Finalization of Project

title: AWS Community Day (30/05/2026)
content:
AWS Community Day Report — 30/05/2026 Event Purpose Share AWS, AI, and soft-skills knowledge within the First Cloud Journey / FCAJ community. Showcase hackathon projects and challenger products. Connect learners at the AWS Vietnam office. Event Information Event Name: AWS Community Day (Event CD AWS) — First Cloud Journey AI Date: 30/05/2026 Location: 26th Floor - AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Event Photos: Google Drive Folder Speakers / Sessions 1. Huynh Thai Linh — Cloud Quest & Floci Level Up Your AWS Skills with Cloud Quest and Floci — learning AWS via Cloud Quest, billing awareness, and local simulation tools (Floci vs LocalStack). 2. Team The Ballers (FCAJ Challenger) — Hackathon & SynthHunter Huynh Anh Khuong, Mai Quoc Anh, Nguyen Tran Minh Quan shared their hackathon journey and demoed SynthHunter — an AI voice verification system that classifies speech as HUMAN / AI GENERATED / NEEDS REVIEW (XLS-R, Whisper, Fourier). 3. Nguyen Thi Quynh Nhu — Why We Always Need Confidence Soft skills: why confidence is a must-have for career and campus life; agenda covering self-doubt, the science behind the struggle, and everyday confidence “hacks”. 4. Nghia Tran — Tu Vi Dai Viet Introduced the Tu Vi Dai Viet product/idea (destiny insights / self-understanding), plus GenAI context and product vision/differentiation. 5. Tran Minh Quan — The Hidden Iceberg of a Project: DevOps Before Disaster A talk on the “hidden iceberg” of a project: adopting a DevOps mindset before disaster — surfacing delivery, operations, and collaboration risks early instead of reacting only after failure. 6. Khac Uy Pham (HGU) — Procrastination A Familiar Enemy / The Iceberg of Procrastination — reframing delay as fear (not laziness), with the closing message “GO BUILD — Starting today”. Group Photo Takeaways & Application AWS Learning Path: Cloud Quest + billing awareness when practicing on a real account (relevant to WebFood deployment). Hackathon & AI Project: How to package a clear problem–solution (SynthHunter) and teamwork under time pressure. Soft Skills: Confidence and anti-procrastination habits — useful for long internship projects. Product / DevOps Thinking: Surface the project’s “hidden iceberg” early (DevOps before disaster), instead of waiting until failure. Event Experience Community Day mixed diverse sessions: AWS learning, hackathon/AI demos, community products, and soft skills. The AWS Bitexco venue plus First Cloud Journey branding made the community atmosphere tangible.
Overall, Event 2 broadened my view beyond the serverless lab — from AWS learning and AI projects to product thinking and soft skills.

title: CloudFront
content:
CloudFront Distribution Create OAC for S3 frontend/media, CloudFront Function webfood-spa-rewrite, distribution with origins:
S3 frontend (default) S3 media (/media/*) API Gateway (/api/*) Next: 5.7.3 Observability.

title: Lambda Functions
content:
Package Lambda Code cd E:\workshop\WebFood\backend npm install --omit=dev Compress-Archive -Path .\* -DestinationPath ..\webfood-lambda.zip -Force Create 5 Lambda Functions Function Handler webfood-api src/lambda.handler webfood-worker src/lambda-worker.handler webfood-ws-connect src/lambda-ws-connect.handler webfood-ws-disconnect src/lambda-ws-disconnect.handler webfood-ws-notify src/lambda-ws-notify.handler Runtime Node.js 20.x, Memory 512 MB, X-Ray Active.
Next: 5.5.3 SQS Trigger.

title: Prerequisites
content:
Before deploying WebFood, prepare the following accounts, tools, and information.
1. AWS Account & Region Sign in to AWS Management Console. Select region us-east-1 (N. Virginia) — required for CloudFront WAF. Note your <ACCOUNT_ID> (12 digits) from the account menu. 2. Local Environment Requirement Details Node.js Version 20+ Git Clone WebFood repository PowerShell / Terminal npm install, Compress-Archive 3. MongoDB Atlas MongoDB Atlas cluster (free tier works), database webappfood. Connection string mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster.xxx.mongodb.net/webappfood. Network Access: add 0.0.0.0/0. 4. MoMo Sandbox partnerCode, accessKey, secretKey Endpoint: https://test-payment.momo.vn/v2/gateway/api/create 5. Amazon SES 1 sender email (FROM_EMAIL) — verify in SES. 1 email for CloudWatch alerts via SNS. 6. IAM Permissions Permissions to create/manage: Lambda, API Gateway, S3, CloudFront, WAF, DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS, Secrets Manager, IAM Role, CloudWatch.
Checklist Region = us-east-1 MongoDB Atlas connection string ready MoMo sandbox credentials SES email verified Node.js 20+ installed WebFood source code (backend/, frontend/) on local machine Next: 5.3 Secrets & Storage.

title: Proposal
content:
In this section, I present the workshop proposal for WebFood — an online food ordering platform deployed on AWS Serverless architecture.
WebFood — Online Food Ordering Platform AWS Serverless Solution for F&B E-Commerce 1. Executive Summary WebFood is an online food ordering platform supporting three user roles: Customer (browse menu, cart, MoMo payment), Merchant (manage products, orders, statistics), and Admin (approve restaurants, vouchers, platform finances). The application is built with React (frontend) and Node.js/Express (backend), with business data stored on MongoDB Atlas.
Within the workshop scope, the project is deployed on AWS using a full serverless architecture: CloudFront + WAF for frontend and media delivery, API Gateway REST + Lambda for APIs, API Gateway WebSocket + DynamoDB for real-time notifications, and EventBridge + SQS + Lambda Worker for asynchronous processing (SES email). The solution is suitable for demo/learning environments with low operating costs and request-based scalability.
2. Problem Statement Current Problem
Traditional food ordering often relies on phone calls, manual chat, or fragmented systems between customers and restaurants. As the number of restaurants and orders grows, tracking order status, sending notifications, and managing vouchers becomes difficult to control. Deploying on fixed servers (EC2) also requires maintenance costs even without traffic.
Solution
WebFood centralizes the ordering flow on a multi-role web platform, combined with MoMo sandbox payment and an event-driven architecture on AWS. When business events occur (order created, status changed, payment succeeded, voucher published), the system publishes events to EventBridge while sending email via SQS/Worker/SES and pushing real-time notifications via WebSocket.
Benefits and ROI
Reduced infrastructure costs through pay-per-use model (Lambda, API Gateway, S3). Separation of static frontend (S3/CloudFront) and API backend (Lambda), easier to maintain and scale. Event-driven design enables feature extension (email, notifications) without modifying core API. Estimated cost of approximately $8–15/month for demo workload (see section 6), significantly lower than maintaining EC2 24/7. 3. Solution Architecture WebFood applies serverless architecture per docs/wedfood.drawio, deployed in us-east-1 region (required for WAF on CloudFront).
AWS Services Used
Group Service Role Edge CloudFront, WAF, CloudFront Functions CDN, protection, SPA routing Storage S3 (frontend + media) Host React build, store product images Compute Lambda (5 functions) Express API, email worker, WebSocket handlers API API Gateway REST + WebSocket REST /api/*, real-time $connect/$disconnect Database DynamoDB Store WebSocket connectionId by userId Messaging EventBridge, SQS + DLQ, SNS Event bus, worker queue, alerts Security Secrets Manager, IAM, OAC JWT/MoMo/MongoDB credentials, bucket policy Observability CloudWatch, X-Ray Logs, alarms, distributed tracing Email SES Order confirmation, password reset Component Design
Frontend: React 19 + Vite, static build uploaded to S3, served via CloudFront. ApiFunction (webfood-api): Wraps Express app via @codegenie/serverless-express, connects to MongoDB Atlas, publishes events to EventBridge. WorkerFunction (webfood-worker): SQS trigger, sends email via SES on OrderCreated, OrderStatusChanged, PaymentSucceeded. WebSocket Lambdas: ws-connect/ws-disconnect manage DynamoDB; ws-notify receives EventBridge events, pushes messages to clients. MongoDB Atlas: Primary database (User, Restaurant, Product, Order, Voucher, etc.) — outside AWS VPC. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases
Local development (Weeks 4–6): React frontend + Express/Mongoose backend, JWT auth, ordering flow, MoMo sandbox integration. AWS architecture design (Weeks 7–8): Draw wedfood.drawio, write SAM template (infra/template.yaml), standardize Lambda handlers. AWS deployment (Weeks 9–10): Manual Console deployment per DEPLOY_CONSOLE.md (or sam deploy) — Secrets Manager, S3, Lambda, API Gateway, CloudFront. Testing & optimization (Weeks 11–12): End-to-end smoke test, enable X-Ray, CloudWatch alarms, update CloudFront/MoMo environment variables. Technical Requirements
Local: Node.js 20+, MongoDB, npm for backend/ and frontend/. AWS: Account with permissions to create Lambda, API Gateway, S3, CloudFront, WAF, EventBridge, DynamoDB, SQS, SES. Third-party: MongoDB Atlas cluster, MoMo Test account, SES-verified email. IaC: AWS SAM (template.yaml) or Console click-ops for workshop reporting purposes. 5. Timeline & Milestones Phase Timeline Key Milestones Learn AWS basics Weeks 1–3 (17/04 – 08/05/2026) Free Tier, EC2, S3, VPC, IAM, CLI Develop WebFood locally Weeks 4–8 (11/05 – 12/06/2026) Learn Lightsail, ELB, CloudWatch; design WebFood Design serverless Weeks 8–9 (08/06 – 19/06/2026) wedfood.drawio, SAM template, Lambda adapter Deploy production Weeks 9–10 (15/06 – 26/06/2026) Console deploy, CloudFront live Testing & reporting Weeks 11–14 (29/06 – 30/07/2026) Smoke test, workshop doc, Hugo site 6. Budget Estimation Estimated monthly cost for demo workload (~10,000 API requests, ~5 GB CloudFront transfer):
Service Estimated Cost/Month AWS Lambda (5 functions) ~$1.50 API Gateway REST + WebSocket ~$2.00 Amazon CloudFront ~$2.50 Amazon S3 (frontend + media) ~$0.50 DynamoDB (on-demand) ~$0.25 EventBridge + SQS ~$0.10 AWS WAF ~$5.00 Amazon SES ~$0.10 Secrets Manager ~$0.40 Total ~$12.35/month (~$148/year) View details on AWS Pricing Calculator.
Or download budget estimation file.
7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix
Risk Impact Probability Lambda cold start Medium Medium MongoDB Atlas connection loss High Low MoMo sandbox unresponsive Medium Low WAF/CloudFront cost exceeds Free Tier Medium Medium Lambda zip > 50 MB (node_modules) Medium Medium Mitigation Strategies
Provisioned concurrency for webfood-api if needed (production). MongoDB Atlas: IP whitelist, connection pool monitoring. CloudWatch billing alarm + SNS notification. npm install --omit=dev when packaging Lambda; upload via S3 if zip is large. Contingency Plan
Fallback to local mode (Express + MongoDB) when AWS has issues during demo. SAM template (infra/template.yaml) for quick stack recreation after teardown. 8. Expected Outcomes Technical Improvements
End-to-end ordering flow: customer places order → API Lambda → EventBridge → SES email + WebSocket voucher toast. Centralized administration: admin approves restaurants, merchant updates menu, customer tracks orders in real-time. Scalable architecture: add new event types on EventBridge without API refactoring. Long-term Value
Reference platform for serverless e-commerce projects on AWS. Comprehensive hands-on experience: IaC (SAM), event-driven, CDN, WebSocket, observability. Can extend to production with Cognito auth, CI/CD pipeline, and multi-region.

title: S3 Buckets
content:
S3 Buckets Console: S3 → Create bucket (create 2 buckets)
Bucket Name Notes Frontend webfood-frontend-<ACCOUNT_ID>-prod Block Public Access on, access via CloudFront OAC Media webfood-media-<ACCOUNT_ID>-prod Add CORS for GET CloudFront bucket policy will be added in 5.7.2 CloudFront.

content:

Next: 5.4.3 EventBridge & SNS.

title: GameDay: 8 Teams Battle (20/06/2026)
content:
GameDay: 8 Teams Battle Report (20/06/2026) Event Purpose Create an entertaining playground and challenge AWS knowledge in a fun way, strengthening connections within the community. Reinforce knowledge about the AWS ecosystem through practical questions and hypothetical scenarios. Event Information Event Name: Event GameDay 8 Đội Tranh Hùng (GameDay: 8 Teams Battle) Date: 20/06/2026 Location: 26th Floor - AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Event Photos: Google Drive Folder Teams and Hosts List Below is the list of competing teams in GameDay.
Hosts List Opening the competition, Mr. Long warmed up the atmosphere by introducing the rules in detail and directly coordinating the first match. To help all audiences easily follow the progress, he read and supported the direct translation of the questions into Vietnamese. His smooth guidance, from presenting challenges to announcing scores and results, contributed to a dramatic and fulfilling match. Following the program, Mr. Hoang Hieu, Mr. Thinh Nguyen, and Mr. Duy Khiem took on the role of leading the next matches. Their enthusiasm was clearly demonstrated through each question and the encouraging applause for the teams after each turn. The highlight of this part was how the hosts explained the answers extremely carefully and easily, helping all audiences grasp the knowledge. In addition, they flexibly created interaction by giving clever hints and inviting the audience to directly support the teams. In particular, the audiences who gave correct answers received attractive gifts from the FCAJ organizers. Competing Teams 1. Pair 1 2. Pair 2 3. Pair 3 4. Pair 4 Key Highlights Dramatic AWS Arena: The head-to-head competition between 8 teams revolving around the AWS ecosystem created thrilling and fascinating score chases. Dedicated Guidance from Hosts: Mr. Long, Hoang Hieu, Thinh Nguyen, and Duy Khiem not only coordinated smoothly but also acted as knowledge transmitters. The direct translation and meticulous explanation of each answer turned every question into a highly accessible practical lesson. Explosive Interaction: The audience was not left behind thanks to the cleverness of the hosts who continuously gave hints and invited the audience to “rescue” the teams. This interaction, along with gifts from FCAJ, pushed the heat of the hall to a climax. Takeaways & Application Systematizing Cloud Knowledge: Identified my own knowledge gaps and reinforced core AWS services through practical hypothetical scenarios. Training Combat Mindset: Learned many troubleshooting tips and flexible, agile approaches from the in-depth explanations of the experts. Practical Application: Applied the mindset of designing network architecture and server infrastructure on AWS to optimize the deployment of ongoing software projects, typically the online food ordering application or internal management systems. The knowledge from the event helps shape how to configure the system to be more stable, secure, and load-bearing. Event Experience The atmosphere at the 26th Floor - AWS Vietnam Office was truly explosive and full of energy. Watching the head-to-head matches brought suspense, while also stimulating reflex thinking as I, in the audience seat, was also drawn into reasoning for the answers under time pressure. The feeling of being immersed in a tech environment that is both fiercely competitive and extremely friendly and open was a wonderful experience.
The GameDay event is not simply an entertaining playground but truly a miniature “training ground” to hone agile thinking and reinforce practical AWS knowledge. Through the event, I not only gained more professional values but also clearly felt the strong spirit of cohesion of the FCAJ community, thereby gaining tremendous motivation to continue developing on the path of pursuing Cloud technology.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section lists the blog posts I published in the AWS Study Group VN group during the First Cloud AI Journey program.
Blog 1 — AI Agents on Amazon Bedrock & AgentCore A post sharing perspectives on AI Agent architecture on AWS: AI is not just a chatbot — it can understand goals, plan, use tools, and perform real work through Amazon Bedrock / AgentCore. Key themes include multi-agent design, tool use, and AI as a “worker” inside the system.

title: EventBridge & SNS
content:
EventBridge Custom Event Bus Console: EventBridge → Event buses → Create event bus
Field Value Name webfood-events-prod Click Create. 📌 Note the bus ARN (arn:aws:events:us-east-1:<ACCOUNT_ID>:event-bus/webfood-events-prod).
Do not create Rules yet — Lambda webfood-ws-notify does not exist. Configure rules in 5.6.1 EventBridge Rules.
SNS Alert Topic Console: SNS → Topics → Create topic
Field Value Type Standard Name webfood-alarms-prod Click Create topic. 📌 Note the ARN.
Subscriptions tab → Create subscription:
Field Value Protocol Email Endpoint your CloudWatch alert email Click Create subscription → open email → Confirm subscription (required to receive alerts).
Next: 5.5 Lambda.

title: Observability & SES
content:
Update Secrets Manager After CloudFront domain is ready, update secret webfood/prod/app-secrets:
MOMO_REDIRECT_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>/payment-result MOMO_IPN_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>/api/payment/momo/ipn FRONTEND_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain> Lambda webfood-api → Environment variables → MEDIA_CDN_URL = https://<cloudfront-domain>.
Force new cold start: re-save Lambda environment variables so cached secrets refresh.
CloudWatch Alarms Console: CloudWatch → Alarms → Create alarm (3 times). Each alarm notifies SNS topic webfood-alarms-prod from 5.4.3.
Alarm 1 — webfood-api errors Metric: Lambda → webfood-api → Errors, Statistic Sum, Period 5 minutes Condition: Greater/Equal 1 Missing data: Treat missing data as good Notification: SNS webfood-alarms-prod Name: webfood-api-errors-prod Alarm 2 — webfood-worker errors Same as Alarm 1, change Function → webfood-worker, alarm name → webfood-worker-errors-prod.
Alarm 3 — DLQ messages Metric: SQS → webfood-order-events-dlq-prod → ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible Statistic: Maximum, Period 5 minutes Condition: Greater/Equal 1 Notification: SNS webfood-alarms-prod Name: webfood-dlq-messages-prod Verify SES SES → Verified identities → verify FROM_EMAIL and test recipient emails (SES Sandbox only sends to verified addresses).
Next: 5.7.4 Frontend Deploy.

title: Secrets & Storage
content:
Create Secrets Manager (app configuration) and S3 buckets (frontend + media). These resources are independent of Lambda — create them first.
Sub-sections 5.3.1 Secrets Manager 5.3.2 S3 Buckets 📌 Note the secret ARN and bucket names — later steps (IAM, Lambda, CloudFront) will need them.

title: SQS Trigger
content:
SQS Trigger for Worker webfood-worker → Add trigger → SQS webfood-order-events-prod, batch size 5, enable Report batch item failures.
Next: 5.6 API & Events.

title: STUDY TOUR: AWS — ENTERPRISE CLOUD ARCHITECTURES & INDUSTRY APPLICATIONS (04/07/2026)
content:
STUDY TOUR: AWS — ENTERPRISE CLOUD ARCHITECTURES & INDUSTRY APPLICATIONS Report (04/07/2026) Event Purpose Market Update and Career Orientation: Provide an overview of recruitment trends in the Cloud Computing field and outline career development paths on a global scale. Narrowing the “Theory and Practice” Gap: Guide how to transform theoretical knowledge from the classroom into practical technological solutions, helping candidates score absolute points with employers. Sharing Practical Experience from Experts: Bring authentic perspectives on the journey of transforming from students to data engineers, and analyze how enterprises optimize real-world data architectures led by top experts from AWS, Renova Cloud, and Cloud Kinetics. Inspiring and Nurturing Talent: In-depth discussions (via Fireside Chat) on strategies to nurture and develop the next generation of breakthrough tech talents in Vietnam. Event Information Event Name: STUDY TOUR: AWS — ENTERPRISE CLOUD ARCHITECTURES & INDUSTRY APPLICATIONS Date: 04/07/2026 Location: 26th Floor - AWS Vietnam Office (Bitexco Tower, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City) Role: Attendee Event Photos: Facebook Link Speakers / Sessions 1. Mr. Nguyen Gia Hung — Job Market Trends & Career Trends Mr. Nguyen Gia Hung brought an overview of the job market and the latest career trends today. The session focused on updating prominent recruitment trends in the Cloud Computing field, while outlining global career development paths. In particular, he shared an inspiring story about the journey “from the lecture hall to the battlefield” — the process of transforming from a student to a real data engineer, along with deep insights into how businesses optimize data architecture in a real environment. 2. Ms. Nhu Tran — Survival Skills & Workplace Culture Not stopping at professional knowledge, the event also equipped attendees with essential soft skills through the lens of Ms. Nhu Tran. 3. Mr. Khang Nguyen — AI Ready Freshers AI-Ready Freshers: An inspiring sharing session combining the practical learning journey and career orientation in the AI era. Mr. Khang Nguyen emphasized equipping an agile mindset, technology mastery skills, and quick adaptability to help freshers confidently break through and score points with employers. 4. Mr. Vinh Banh — Renova Cloud, Cloud Kinetics The sharing session focused on core knowledge such as programming, databases, data structures and algorithms, distributed systems, APIs, and necessary technology platforms before learning Cloud & AI. In addition, Mr. Vinh Banh introduced the skill development roadmap for Cloud engineers and how to transform fundamental knowledge into practical Cloud applications. Group Photo Key Highlights Overview of Cloud & Data: Mr. Nguyen Gia Hung’s sharing brought a realistic view of the job market, global recruitment trends, and the transformation path from student to a real Data Engineer. Office Survival Skills: Ms. Nhu Tran equipped attendees with Survival Skills and how to understand corporate culture, helping students become more confident and tactful when entering a professional working environment. “AI-Ready Freshers” Mindset: Mr. Khang Nguyen guided how to build an agile mindset, technology mastery skills, and quick adaptability to leverage the power of AI, thereby creating a great competitive advantage in the eyes of employers. Transforming Foundation into Cloud Architecture: Mr. Vinh Banh emphasized the importance of core knowledge (Databases, APIs, Distributed Systems, Programming) and guided how to assemble these “bricks” into enterprise-scale Cloud solutions (Enterprise Architectures). Takeaways & Application Optimizing Practical Project Architecture: Understand the importance of mastering foundational knowledge before deploying Cloud. The knowledge of distributed systems and API design from the event will be directly applied to optimizing databases and backend processing flows (using Node.js, Java Spring Boot) for ongoing projects like the WebFood platform, ensuring the system is resilient and easy to scale. Integrating AI Mindset into Software Development: Apply the “AI-Ready” mindset by constantly updating new technologies, researching the integration of AI solutions (like Google Gemini) into web/app projects to improve chatbot features, enhance user experience, and create technical highlights. Equipping Soft Skills and Work Discipline: Apply “survival skills” and office culture to improve team communication skills. At the same time, maintain strict discipline in source code management, task division on GitHub/GitLab to get used to enterprise-standard work styles right from school. Event Experience The event took place in the professional space of the AWS Vietnam office (Bitexco Tower) and truly brought an overwhelming and inspiring experience. Listening to the “from lecture hall to battlefield” stories of the speakers not only helped bridge the gap between theory and practice but also provided a very clear career path. The open, dynamic atmosphere at the event added tremendous motivation to constantly hone programming skills, ready for the challenges in the volatile world of Cloud Computing and AI.
The STUDY TOUR: AWS (04/07/2026 at Bitexco) event is a bridge to help students transform classroom knowledge into practical combat capability. Gathering a team of experts from AWS, Renova Cloud, and Cloud Kinetics, the program brings a complete picture of recruitment trends, office skills, AI-Ready mindset, and how to design enterprise-scale Cloud architectures.

title: Data & Messaging
content:
Create data storage and async messaging services: DynamoDB (WebSocket connections), SQS (order queue), EventBridge (event bus), SNS (alerts).
Sub-sections 5.4.1 DynamoDB 5.4.2 SQS 5.4.3 EventBridge & SNS

title: Frontend Deploy
content:
Build & Upload Frontend Create .env in frontend/:
VITE_API_URL=https://<cloudfront-domain>/api VITE_WS_URL=wss://<ws-api-id>.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/prod cd E:\workshop\WebFood\frontend npm run build aws s3 sync dist/ s3://webfood-frontend-<ACCOUNT_ID>-prod/ --delete Next: 5.8 Smoke Test.

Teams were divided into 4 knockout pairs, challenging their understanding of the AWS ecosystem. The spectacular score chases were not only to win the prestigious ticket to the next round but also to conquer extremely attractive “loot” from the organizers.
Value Gained: Systematized knowledge, identified gaps, and quickly reinforced knowledge about AWS services through real-world scenarios, trained combat mindset, improved analytical ability and quick reflexes under time pressure, broadened perspectives, and learned flexible problem-solving approaches from other teams.
→ View detailed report for Event 4

title: Lambda
content:
Create IAM Role, package code, deploy 5 Lambda functions, and attach SQS trigger to Worker.
Sub-sections 5.5.1 IAM Role 5.5.2 Lambda Functions 5.5.3 SQS Trigger

title: Workshop
content:
Deploy WebFood on AWS Serverless This workshop guides you through deploying the complete WebFood architecture on AWS Management Console (click-ops) with screenshots for each step. Sections are grouped by function for clarity.
Live demo (for reviewers): https://dh9xhc3jrccu4.cloudfront.net/
Reviewers can open this URL to explore the WebFood system deployed on AWS.
Required region: us-east-1 (WAF for CloudFront must be created in us-east-1).
Table of Contents # Section Contents 5.1 Overview Goals, architecture, main flows 5.2 Prerequisites Accounts, tools, checklist 5.3 Secrets & Storage Secrets Manager, S3 5.4 Data & Messaging DynamoDB, SQS, EventBridge, SNS 5.5 Lambda IAM, package code, functions, trigger 5.6 API & Events EventBridge rules, REST API, WebSocket 5.7 CDN & Operations WAF, CloudFront, CloudWatch, SES, frontend 5.8 Smoke Test Verify main application flows 5.9 Teardown Delete resources to avoid ongoing costs

title: API & Events
content:
Configure EventBridge rules (event routing) and create API Gateway REST + WebSocket.
Sub-sections 5.6.1 EventBridge Rules 5.6.2 REST API 5.6.3 WebSocket API

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Viet Nam Company Limited from 17/04/2026 to 30/07/2026, under the Workforce Bootcamp – First Cloud AI Journey program, I had the opportunity to learn AWS cloud fundamentals and apply them in a real project.
I designed and deployed WebFood — an online food-ordering platform on an AWS Serverless architecture (CloudFront, WAF, S3, API Gateway, Lambda, EventBridge, SQS, DynamoDB, Secrets Manager, SES, CloudWatch). Through this work I improved full-stack development skills (React, Node.js/Express), cloud architecture design, console-based (click-ops) deployment, workshop documentation, and technical reporting.
Regarding work ethic, I aimed to complete weekly worklog tasks on time, followed program processes, and proactively asked mentors when I faced difficulties configuring AWS services.
To reflect objectively on the internship, I evaluate myself against the criteria below:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting work clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and participating in teams ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, recognition from the team ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Needs Improvement Strengthen discipline and strictly comply with the rules and regulations of the company or any organization Improve problem-solving thinking Enhance communication skills in both daily interactions and professional contexts, including handling situations effectively

title: CDN & Operations
content:
Final deploy phase: WAF, CloudFront, update secrets, CloudWatch, SES, and upload frontend.
Sub-sections 5.7.1 WAF 5.7.2 CloudFront 5.7.3 Observability 5.7.4 Frontend Deploy

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Here, you can freely share your personal opinions about your experience participating in the First Cloud AI Journey program. This will help the FCAJ team improve any shortcomings based on the following aspects:
Overall Evaluation 1. Working Environment
The working environment is very friendly and open. FCAJ members are always willing to help whenever I encounter difficulties, even outside working hours. The workspace is tidy and comfortable, helping me focus better. However, I think it would be nice to have more social gatherings or team bonding activities to strengthen relationships.
2. Support from Mentor / Team Admin
The mentor provides very detailed guidance, explains clearly when I don’t understand, and always encourages me to ask questions. The admin team supports administrative tasks, provides necessary documents, and creates favorable conditions for me to work effectively. I especially appreciate that the mentor allows me to try and solve problems myself instead of just giving the answer.
3. Relevance of Work to Academic Major
The tasks I was assigned align well with the knowledge I learned at university, while also introducing me to new areas I had never encountered before. This allowed me to both strengthen my foundational knowledge and gain practical skills.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
During the internship, I learned many new skills such as using project management tools, teamwork skills, and professional communication in a corporate environment. The mentor also shared valuable real-world experiences that helped me better plan my career path.
5. Company Culture & Team Spirit
The company culture is very positive: everyone respects each other, works seriously but still keeps things enjoyable. When there are urgent projects, everyone works together and supports one another regardless of their position. This made me feel like a real part of the team, even as an intern.
6. Internship Policies / Benefits
The company provides an internship allowance and offers flexible working hours when needed. In addition, having the opportunity to join internal training sessions is a big plus.
Additional Questions What did you find most satisfying during your internship?
I was most satisfied with the opportunity to directly participate in real-world projects, particularly working with technologies like AWS Serverless and ReactJS. The enthusiastic support from my mentor is also something I deeply appreciate. What do you think the company should improve for future interns?
I think the company could organize regular internal seminars or tech talks specifically for interns, allowing everyone to interact and share knowledge with each other. If recommending to a friend, would you suggest they intern here? Why or why not?
Absolutely yes. This is a fantastic environment that not only helps IT students strengthen practical programming skills but also fosters a professional working attitude. Suggestions & Expectations Do you have any suggestions to improve the internship experience?
It would be great to have a more detailed onboarding guide during the first week, covering the standard tools and workflows of the projects so newcomers can integrate faster. Would you like to continue this program in the future?
I would love to continue my journey and contribute to the company as a full-time employee if the opportunity arises after this internship. Any other comments (free sharing):
Thank you to the company and especially the mentors in the FCAJ team for providing the best conditions for me to complete this internship with many memorable experiences!

title: Smoke Test
content:
Smoke Test Access CloudFront URL and verify main flows:
1. Frontend Open https://<cloudfront-domain>/ → WebFood homepage displays. Register / login as customer. 2. REST API Browse menu, add items to cart. Place order → verify order appears in history. 3. MoMo Payment (sandbox) Select MoMo payment → sandbox redirect → return to app. 4. Event-driven After placing order: check SES email (if configured). Admin publishes voucher → client receives toast via WebSocket. 5. Merchant / Admin Login as merchant → manage products, update order status. Login as admin → approve restaurants, manage vouchers. Next: 5.9 Teardown.

title: Teardown
content:
Teardown — Resource Cleanup When no longer needed (avoid ongoing costs), delete in reverse deploy order:
CloudFront Distribution (disable → delete, wait for deployed) WAF WebACL API Gateway REST + WebSocket Lambda functions (5) EventBridge rules + event bus SQS queues + DLQ DynamoDB table SNS topic + subscriptions S3 buckets (must be empty first) Secrets Manager secret IAM Role webfood-lambda-role-prod CloudWatch Alarms Note: MongoDB Atlas cluster is outside AWS — delete separately on Atlas if not needed.
Workshop Conclusion After this workshop, you have successfully deployed the WebFood platform with a complete AWS Serverless architecture: CDN, API, real-time WebSocket, event-driven email, and observability.
